---
title: "Methodology"
subtitle: "Three-Layer Small Area Estimation Framework"
---

## Overview

Standard small area estimation methods (e.g., Elbers, Lanjouw & Lanjouw 2003) rely on linear models and parametric assumptions that may fail to capture the complex, nonlinear relationships between household characteristics and welfare. Our approach replaces this with a three-layer framework that is distribution-free, spatially coherent, and calibrated to survey benchmarks.

## The Pipeline

::: {.pipeline-flow}
::: {.pipeline-box}
Survey + Census<br/>Harmonization
:::
[&rarr;]{.pipeline-arrow}
::: {.pipeline-box}
Layer 1: DRF<br/>Distributional RF
:::
[&rarr;]{.pipeline-arrow}
::: {.pipeline-box}
Layer 2: BYM2<br/>Spatial Smoothing
:::
[&rarr;]{.pipeline-arrow}
::: {.pipeline-box}
Layer 3: OT<br/>Calibration
:::
:::

---

## Layer 1: Distributional Random Forests (DRF)

::: {.method-step}
#### What it does
DRF (Cevid et al., 2022) estimates the full conditional distribution of per capita household income, not just the mean. For each census household, DRF produces a set of adaptive nearest-neighbor weights from the survey, yielding a nonparametric estimate of $F(y \mid X)$.
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### Key features
- **2,000 trees** with honesty (separate estimation and splitting samples)
- **43 harmonized predictors** covering demographics, education, employment, housing, and assets
- Outputs: $\hat{P}(\text{poor} \mid X)$, $\hat{FGT}_1$, $\hat{FGT}_2$, conditional mean and variance
- Out-of-bag CRPS and calibration diagnostics
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### Municipal aggregation
Area-level estimates are simple averages of household-level predictions:
$$\hat{\theta}_a^{DRF} = \frac{1}{N_a} \sum_{i \in a} \hat{P}(\text{poor} \mid X_i)$$
with variance estimated from the conditional variance of individual predictions.
:::

---

## Layer 2: BYM2 Spatial Smoothing

::: {.method-step}
#### What it does
The Besag-York-Molli&eacute; 2 model (Riebler et al., 2016) smooths the DRF area estimates by borrowing strength from neighboring municipalities and accounting for estimation uncertainty.
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### Model specification
Two competing formulations:

**Gaussian Fay-Herriot** (known variance):
$$\hat{\theta}_a \mid \theta_a \sim N(\theta_a, \hat{\psi}_a), \quad \theta_a = \mathbf{x}_a'\boldsymbol{\beta} + u_a + v_a$$

**Beta BYM2** (for proportions on [0,1]):
$$\hat{\theta}_a \sim \text{Beta}(\mu_a \phi, (1-\mu_a)\phi), \quad \text{logit}(\mu_a) = \mathbf{x}_a'\boldsymbol{\beta} + u_a + v_a$$

Model selection via WAIC. Adjacency from GADM 4.1 administrative boundaries.
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### What it adds
- Spatial coherence: neighboring municipalities inform each other
- Shrinkage: noisy estimates from small municipalities are stabilized
- Posterior credible intervals with proper uncertainty quantification
:::

---

## Layer 3: Optimal Transport Calibration

::: {.method-step}
#### What it does
OT calibration (Villani, 2009) maps the DRF-predicted welfare distribution to match the survey-observed distribution at the regional level, correcting any systematic bias in the imputed distribution's shape.
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### Implementation
1. **Quantile mapping**: Build monotone transport map $T$ from predicted to observed quantiles on a 999-point grid
2. **Regularization**: $T_\lambda = (1-\lambda)T + \lambda \cdot \text{Id}$, with $\lambda$ chosen by cross-validation
3. **Regional calibration**: Separate maps for each of 18 department $\times$ area domains (minimum 30 survey HH)
4. **Raking**: Final municipal estimates raked so department totals match OT-calibrated aggregates
:::

::: {.method-step}
#### Final estimates
$$\hat{\theta}_a^{final} = \hat{\theta}_a^{BYM2} \times \frac{\hat{\theta}_d^{OT}}{\hat{\theta}_d^{BYM2}}$$
where $d$ indexes departments. This preserves within-department spatial patterns from BYM2 while ensuring consistency with calibrated regional totals.
:::

---

## Uncertainty Quantification

::: {.method-step}
#### Split conformal prediction
- 80/20 train/calibration split with re-trained DRF
- Basic and adaptive conformity scores
- 90% and 80% conformal prediction intervals
- Coefficient of variation classification: High (&lt;10%), Medium (10-20%), Moderate (20-30%), Low (&gt;30%)
:::

---

## Data Harmonization

43 predictors harmonized across survey and census:

| Domain | Variables |
|--------|-----------|
| **Demographics** | Head age, sex, indigenous status, mother tongue, marital status, HH size, dependency ratio |
| **Education** | Head years of education, literacy |
| **Employment** | Head sector (agriculture/industry/services), employment status |
| **Housing** | Wall, roof, floor materials; water, sanitation, electricity, cooking fuel |
| **Assets** | PCA-based asset index from 15 durable goods |
| **Composition** | Share children, elderly, working-age; max education in HH |

## References

- Cevid, D., Michel, L., Meinshausen, N., & B&uuml;hlmann, P. (2022). Distributional random forests. *JRSS-B*.
- Elbers, C., Lanjouw, J.O., & Lanjouw, P. (2003). Micro-level estimation of poverty and inequality. *Econometrica*.
- Riebler, A., S&oslash;rbye, S.H., Simpson, D., & Rue, H. (2016). An intuitive Bayesian spatial model for disease mapping. *Statistical Methods in Medical Research*.
- Villani, C. (2009). *Optimal Transport: Old and New*. Springer.
